Typical range for industrial 1064 nm fiber lasers cleaning reflective metals like zinc to avoid excessive heat buildup; starting at 150 W balances efficiency and safety. Max limited to prevent melting (zinc MP ~419°C). Source: CleanLaser and IPG Photonics equipment specs for metal surface preparation.

Range ensures effective contaminant removal without substrate ablation; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oils), faster for light rust. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal zinc cleaning to minimize thermal effects due to high reflectivity. Source: Industry standards from Laserax and academic reviews in J. Laser Appl. (2018).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective industrial cleaning of zinc; shorter pulses (ps/fs) reduce HAZ but are less common at 1064 nm. 50 ns recommended for minimal heat diffusion in zinc's high thermal conductivity (~116 W/mK). Source: Laser physics studies in Opt. Express (2015) on metal ablation thresholds.

Typical for pulsed fiber lasers; higher rates speed up cleaning but increase heat accumulation on reflective zinc, risking damage. 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature in Appl. Surf. Sci. (2020) on pulse train effects for metal cleaning.

Fluence range for contaminant ablation without zinc damage; threshold ~2.5 J/cm² due to high reflectivity at 1064 nm. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe removal of oxides/contaminants. Conservative to account for zinc's low melting point. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. (2017).

Smaller spots for precision on zinc details, larger for broad areas; 200 μm recommended for uniform energy distribution and reduced peak fluence on reflective surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for zinc to ensure complete removal without cumulative heating. Source: Industry case studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2019) on multi-pass laser cleaning of metals.

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or overexposure; 60% optimal for zinc to prevent uneven heating on large areas. Source: Process optimization in Laser Technol. J. (2021), typical for industrial scanning parameters.
